text: I am sharing a sigmoid colectomy for a smoldering diverticulitis in a patient who had never prior had a colonoscopy. Symptoms have been really persistent for about one year. The last known attack was about six months earlier, but she just never really felt better after that attack. And CAT scans that she had had intermittently over that amount of time always showed persistent diverticular disease. And at one time she had an intramural abscess. Because of her symptoms and never really being able to get things under control, she didn't have a colonoscopy and opted for surgery. She had lost close to sixty pounds during this last year.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection despite the adherence of the phlegmon to the lateral sidewall, using it as a self-retaining retractor. A high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is conducted, and medial dissection is continued towards the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) near the ligament of Treitz. The surgeon assesses the need for splenic flexure mobilization and plans to proceed with lateral mobilization if necessary.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the diseased area, utilizing ICG in the ureters for enhanced visualization with Firefly technology. After completing the medial dissection, a sponge is placed to maintain exposure, and lateral dissection begins higher on the descending colon to access a virgin plane. The surgeon meets the previously dissected medial plane, removing mesenteric attachments from the girotis fascia towards the splenic flexure. As the dissection approaches the flexure, the vessel sealer is used for improved hemostatic control and grasping functionality.

The surgeon performs lateral and superior mobilization of the splenic flexure without changing patient position or redocking. The mesentery is detached from the superior aspect of Giroud's fascia, progressing around the flexure. The pancreas is visible as the surgeon navigates the natural plane.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the splenic flexure using a super mesocolic approach, detaching the greater omentum from the distal transverse colon and splenic flexure. The patient remains in steep Trendelenburg position with the left side elevated. The flexure is fully mobilized, and the surgeon proceeds to take down the phlegmon using an inferior approach, employing a suction device for smoke clearance and blunt dissection. The sigmoid colon is separated from the lateral sidewall, joining the medial dissection, with a sponge placed to protect the ureter.

The surgeon completes the rectosigmoid dissection with good exposure, performing a distal rectal mobilization in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane. The rectal mesentery is taken, allowing the rectum to be lifted out of the pelvis. The proximal mesentery is dissected up to the mid descending colon without taking a high ligation of the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV).

The surgeon checks the bowel length and marks the transection site with cautery for perfusion assessment. They proceed with distal and proximal bowel transections using scissors, opting for a hand-sewn anastomosis instead of staplers. Three posterior 3-0 silk sutures are placed to invert the posterior lip, with additional sutures for retraction, as the surgeon uses two 3-0 V-Loc sutures starting posteriorly in the midline.

The surgeon performs rectal mobilization, lifting the rectum out of the pelvis for better visualization and angles. Sutures are carefully placed from the posterior midline, transitioning from posterior to anterior, ensuring precise placement to achieve an inverted anterior and posterior lip. The surgeon uses a canal technique to lay sutures over the top, maintaining the inverted lip configuration.

The surgeon ties the sutures to complete the anastomosis and performs a leak test using a nineteen millimeter proctoscope. The proctoscope is inserted up to the anastomosis, showing a widely patent connection with no visible leak.
